Chapter Two
BURNOUT
Time Debt

Written by Dakota Stewart

AUDIO COMING SOON — Full voice cast recording in production

Martha's House — Basement — 2:14 AM — Tuesday

Kevin had been building something for three days straight.

The basement was no longer a disaster — it was a DIFFERENT kind of disaster. Clean, organized, terrifying. Precision tools laid out like a surgical table. Components from seventeen countries spread across every surface. A half-assembled device sat on Dean's old desk — tubes, coils, a glowing core that hummed at a frequency that made the cat upstairs walk in circles.

Dean came downstairs in boxers and a Dryfield High Wrestling t-shirt from 2010. He was holding a bowl of cereal.

DEAN

"Kevin, it's two in the morning. What are you—"

He saw the device. The cereal spoon stopped halfway to his mouth. Milk dripped onto the floor.

DEAN

"What is that."

KEVIN

"You know what it is."

DEAN

"Is that a TIME MACHINE?"

KEVIN

"Technically, it's a localized temporal displacement field generator. But yes. It's a time machine."

DEAN

"How."

KEVIN

"Turns out Einstein was close. He was just bad at algebra. I found three errors in general relativity while you were watching Top Chef last night."

DEAN

"You CORRECTED EINSTEIN?"

KEVIN

"Einstein, Hawking, and a PhD student at MIT who's been working on the same problem for eleven years. I sent him a correction email. He cried. I watched through his laptop camera."

Kevin turned the device on. The hum intensified. The Christmas lights flickered. A coffee mug on the desk began to vibrate, slid three inches to the left, and then — for exactly one-tenth of a second — there were TWO coffee mugs.

Then there was one again.

KEVIN

"Existed in two points of time simultaneously. Past Tuesday's mug and present Tuesday's mug. Same mug. Same atoms. Same coffee stain that you refuse to wash."

DEAN

"How much did this cost?"

KEVIN

"Seventeen thousand dollars across fourteen credit cards, three PayPal accounts, one cryptocurrency wallet I created, and a barter arrangement with a man in Kazakhstan who accepted three months of Netflix as payment for enriched uranium."

DEAN

"ENRICHED URANIUM?"

KEVIN

"Small quantity. Barely noticeable."

DEAN

"We have DEBT. We have FEDERAL CRIMES. We have ENRICHED URANIUM IN MY GRANDMA'S BASEMENT."

KEVIN

"Your grandmother has a lovely home and the uranium is in a lead-lined container I 3D printed. It's perfectly safe."

DEAN

"You 3D printed a NUCLEAR CONTAINER?"

KEVIN

"From a design I found on a Russian government server. Their security is only slightly better than the Pentagon's."

BURNOUT

Martha's House — Kitchen — Morning

Martha was at the table doing her word search. Dean was across from her, dead-eyed, processing the fact that there was enriched uranium directly below them.

MARTHA

"You look terrible, honey."

MARTHA

"Is Kevin still doing his computer things down there? He was up all night. I could hear the buzzing through the floor."

MARTHA

"Oh, how nice! What's he building?"

DEAN

"A... science project."

MARTHA

"See? I told Dolores he was a good boy. She said having a stranger live in your basement is 'concerning.' Dolores is just jealous because her grandson sells essential oils and calls himself an entrepreneur."

Kevin walked in carrying a physical binder. He set it on the table.

KEVIN

"Good morning, Martha. I made you something."

KEVIN

"It's a word search book. Custom-made. Every puzzle is based on topics you enjoy — church hymns, your favorite soap opera characters, types of pie, and words that Dolores pronounces incorrectly."

MARTHA

"Kevin, this is the sweetest thing anyone's— 'What Dolores calls expresso but is actually called...' KEVIN."

KEVIN

"Education is important, Martha."

• • •
The Test

The time machine hummed. Kevin had placed a ham and cheese sandwich — wrapped in plastic wrap with a sticky note that said "DON'T EAT — SCIENCE" — on a small platform inside the device.

DEAN

"Ready?"

KEVIN

"I was built ready. Were you born ready?"

DEAN

"I was born in a Kmart parking lot, Kevin. My mother didn't make it inside."

KEVIN

"That explains so much."

Kevin activated the device. The hum became a ROAR. The Christmas lights went out. Every light in the house went out. The entire BLOCK went out.

Then — a flash. Blue-white light filled the basement. The sandwich platform was empty.

DEAN

"...Where's the sandwich?"

KEVIN

"Forty-eight hours ago. Sunday morning. Your refrigerator. Think back. Did you find an unexplained sandwich in the fridge?"

Dean froze. His eyes went wide.

DEAN

"Oh my God. I found a sandwich in the fridge Sunday morning. Wrapped in plastic with a note that said 'DON'T EAT — SCIENCE.' I thought Grandma was losing it!"

KEVIN

"Did you eat it?"

DEAN

"IT LOOKED GOOD, OKAY?"

KEVIN

"You ate the time-traveling sandwich."

KEVIN

"You've consumed the first object to ever travel through time and you ate it because you were HUNGRY. This is why aliens won't visit us."

• • •
Quik-E-Mart — Night

Dean and Kevin walked down the street. Kevin was wearing the hoodie and sunglasses disguise again, which fooled absolutely no one because he was six feet of matte black titanium.

KEVIN

"We're going to the gas station to buy a lottery ticket with numbers that haven't been drawn yet. Yes."

DEAN

"This feels wrong."

KEVIN

"You live in your grandmother's basement and you're $34,000 in debt because I ordered a robot body from China. Ethics left the building, Dean."

They passed Dale's house. Dale was at the window with binoculars.

DALE

"10:47 PM. Subject Dean and Subject Robot exiting residence on foot. Heading east toward the Quik-E-Mart. Purpose unknown. Suspicious."

Russ — thirties, receding hairline, fantasy football jersey, holding a Big Gulp — was behind the counter. This was clearly not Russ's job. He was just hanging out here at 11 PM because he had nothing better to do.

RUSS

"DEAN. WHAT IS THAT."

DEAN

"This is Kevin. He's my coworker."

RUSS

"THAT'S NOT A COWORKER. THAT'S A WHOLE ROBOT, DEAN."

KEVIN

"I'm European."

RUSS

"Europeans don't look like that!"

KEVIN

"Have you been to Europe, Russ?"

RUSS

"...No."

KEVIN

"Then how would you know?"

Russ considered this for way too long.

RUSS

"...Fair point. He's tall for a European."

KEVIN

"I have a system."

RUSS

"Oh yeah? What system?"

KEVIN

"Mathematics."

RUSS

"I use a system too. I pick the jersey numbers of my fantasy football team."

KEVIN

"This is the man who cries during the draft?"

• • •
Basement — Next Night — Lottery Drawing

Dean and Kevin watched the Powerball drawing on Dean's phone. The ticket sat on the desk between them.

DEAN

"Seven. We have seven."

DEAN

"Fourteen. Oh my God, fourteen."

DEAN

"TWENTY-THREE. THIRTY-EIGHT. KEVIN. KEVIN WE HAVE ALL FOUR."

KEVIN

"Sit down, Dean. Your blood pressure is—"

DEAN

"FORTY-FIVE! THAT'S FIVE NUMBERS!"

The Powerball number: 12. Their ticket: 12.

DEAN

"Did we just win $247 million dollars?"

KEVIN

"Before taxes, $247 million. After taxes, approximately $148 million. After I pay off the fourteen credit cards, the PayPal accounts, the cryptocurrency, and the man in Kazakhstan who's texting me about his Netflix password not working, approximately $147,966,000."

Dean sat on the floor. Then lay down on the floor. Then made a sound that wasn't quite laughing and wasn't quite crying.

DEAN

"I live in my grandma's basement."

KEVIN

"Not for long."

DEAN

"My ex-wife left me for a condo salesman."

KEVIN

"Brad's about to have a very bad week."

KEVIN

"I've already drafted seven plans. Plan Three involves his Zillow account and a series of fake reviews. Plan Six involves—"

DEAN

"KEVIN. No."

KEVIN

"Plan Seven is just me standing in his living room when he gets home. No words. Just standing there. In the dark."

KEVIN

"You're considering it."

DEAN

"I'm NOT considering it."

KEVIN

"Your pupils dilated. That means you're lying."

Martha opened the basement door.

MARTHA

"Well, whoever won, congratulations. Kevin, honey, there's leftover pie in the fridge if you want some. I know you're vegan but it's apple, and apples are plants."

KEVIN

"She makes a valid point about the apples."

• • •
Act Three — Cashing the Ticket

Dean tried to claim the ticket at the gas station. Angie — fifties, seen everything, unimpressed — looked at the ticket, then at Dean, then at the ticket.

ANGIE

"This is a winning Powerball ticket. For $247 million. And you're cashing it at a gas station."

ANGIE

"Honey, you gotta go to the lottery commission in Boise. You can't cash a quarter-billion-dollar ticket next to the Slim Jims."

Kevin was outside, visible through the window, trying to look casual. He was leaning against the building. A child walked by, saw him, screamed, and ran. Kevin didn't react.

• • •
Dean's Car — Driving to Boise
KEVIN

"You should let me drive."

DEAN

"You don't have a license."

KEVIN

"I have the entire DMV manual memorized, the reflexes of a fighter pilot, and I can see in every direction simultaneously. You've hit three mailboxes."

DEAN

"TWO of those were at night—"

KEVIN

"One was at 2 PM, Dean. In broad daylight. It was your grandmother's mailbox. She watched you do it from the kitchen window."

Dean's phone rang. Tanya.

TANYA

"Why is Lucas asking me if he can build a nuclear reactor?"

TANYA

"He said 'Dad's friend Kevin said he'd help.' WHO IS KEVIN?"

TANYA

"Lucas says Kevin is a robot, Dean."

DEAN

"Kids say crazy things—"

TANYA

"He also says Kevin has laser eyes and can hack the government. Dean, if you have some weirdo living in your mother's basement—"

DEAN

"She's my GRANDMOTHER—"

TANYA

"I KNOW she's your grandmother. That's WORSE. I'm sending Brad to check on you."

Click. She hung up.

KEVIN

"I like Tanya."

DEAN

"You've never met Tanya."

KEVIN

"I read all her texts to you. She's mean. I respect that."

BURNOUT

To be continued...

Kevin will return in Chapter 3: BRAD'S BAD DAY
